topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink on the paper felt like a physical brand against Leo’s chest. He stared at the 'D-' as if it were a foreign language, the letters swimming into a meaningless blur. He slowly pushed the textbook across the desk, the sound a dull thud in the otherwise quiet room. His jaw was tight, and he picked at a loose thread on his worn hoodie, his gaze fixed on the scuffed floorboards. Every muscle in his neck felt coiled, ready to snap. He didn't bother to look at his roommate, who was laughing loudly at something on his phone, a sound that grated on Leo’s nerves like sandpaper. He just wanted the noise to stop, the pressure to ease, and the weight of that disappointing mark to simply vanish.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" Maya mumbled, the word barely audible, directed at the professor’s signature on the assignment sheet. She traced the edge of the paper with a fingertip, the texture rough beneath her skin. A heavy sigh escaped her lips, a sound more like air escaping a punctured tire than an expression of disappointment. She shoved the paper deep into her backpack, the zipper closing with unnecessary force. Her shoulders slumped forward, pulling her collar up high around her neck. She couldn't bring herself to meet anyone’s eyes, the warmth of the classroom suddenly feeling suffocating. All she could focus on was the dull ache behind her eyes and the insistent drumming of her own heartbeat against her ribs.

[story 3]

He picked up the graded essay, the paper feeling strangely light, almost flimsy. Every sentence seemed to mock him, every poorly constructed argument a testament to his own inadequacy. A slow, deliberate movement took him as he walked toward the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to let a sliver of harsh afternoon light slice across the room. His hands clenched into fists at his sides, the knuckles turning white. He didn't move to tidy up; he just stood there, absorbing the silence, letting the heavy feeling settle deep into his bones. He let out a breath, a long, ragged expulsion of air that seemed to carry all the accumulated frustration of the past few weeks.
